archetype: Real-Time GPR Phase-Shift Anomaly Classifier for Sub-Glacial Basal Meltwater Mapping
description: A field-deployable edge computing system that processes raw Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) data in real-time to detect and classify basal meltwater pockets under moving ice sheets, distinguishing them from bedrock or sediment using phase-shift analysis. Used by polar research expeditions and ice-sheet stability modeling teams, it must operate in extreme cold (-60°C) with high radiation tolerance while processing gigabytes of radar returns per second to prevent data loss during short survey windows.
rules: MUST adhere to the 16-bit GPR trace data format (SEG-Y or proprietary binary) and process sampling rates up to 200 MHz without buffer underruns.
MUST implement hardware-accelerated (FPGA/GPU) Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) pipelines to perform hyperbolic diffraction stacking within 50ms of data acquisition.
MUST support the IEEE 1451 smart transducer interface for asynchronous sensor fusion with GPS/IMU positioning data and surface roughness sensors.
MUST enforce strict deterministic real-time scheduling (RTOS) to prevent jitter that would corrupt phase-coherent radar stacking algorithms.
MUST comply with ISO 19960 (Glaciological Data Standards) for metadata tagging regarding ice velocity, surface elevation, and antenna frequency.
anti_patterns: DO NOT use standard cloud-latency pipelines for initial anomaly detection; the system must function entirely offline due to lack of connectivity in polar regions.

DO NOT apply generic image segmentation AI models (like standard U-Nets) without training on specific dielectric permittivity signatures of cryo-strata, as they fail to distinguish water from wet snow.
DO NOT rely on software-based signal averaging; the thermal noise floor in extreme cold requires analog front-end filtering before digital conversion.
key_technical_challenges: Compensating for variable antenna coupling efficiency caused by rapidly changing snow density and surface roughness in the signal processing chain.
Managing thermal throttling and power budget constraints of high-performance compute modules inside a vacuum-insulated, cryogenic-rated enclosure.
Synchronizing millisecond-level radar pulse timestamps with GPS drift in high-latitude regions where standard GPS accuracy degrades.

recommended_stack.language: Rust (for memory safety in embedded RTOS environments with zero garbage collection pauses)
recommended_stack.framework: Rust-RTIC (Real-Time Interrupt-driven Concurrency) for deterministic multicore scheduling on ARM Cortex-M/RPI Compute Modules
recommended_stack.database_engine: TimeScaleDB (for time-series metadata) paired with HDF5 (for raw binary radar trace storage and archiving)
recommended_stack.cache: Redis (embedded instance) for rapid multi-task data exchange
recommended_stack.message_broker: ZeroMQ (for high-throughput, low-latency inter-process communication between acquisition and inference threads)
special_services: Custom CUDA/OpenCL kernel service for real-time diffraction hyperbola migration
Cryo-Temperature Calibrated Dielectric Constant Lookup Service

compliance_flags: SCAR (Scientific Committee on Antarctic Research) Data Policy
FAIR Data Principles for Cryospheric Sciences
